summary: Gruber explains the financial crisis facing Tapbots (Tweetbot) and The Iconfactory (Twitterrific) after Elon Musk abruptly killed third-party Twitter client access. Both companies face not just lost future revenue but must proactively refund thousands of existing annual subscriptions — money already booked and spent. Both apps released coordinated updates offering subscribers the choice to decline prorated refunds or transfer subscriptions (in Tweetbot's case, to their Mastodon client Ivory). Gruber urges subscribers to reinstall the apps and tap 'I Don't Need a Refund,' emphasizing that 85% of refund costs fall on the small developers, not Apple.
key_points: Twitter's shutdown of third-party clients created a double financial blow: not only did future revenue vanish, but already-collected subscription money must be refunded pro rata, threatening the companies' survival.
The refund mechanics mean Tapbots and The Iconfactory bear 70-85% of refund costs, not Apple — a fact most users likely don't understand.
Both companies released beautifully designed, coordinated farewell updates that prioritize user choice and dignity despite their own financial peril.
Many subscribers who would gladly decline refunds will never see the option because they've already deleted the apps, meaning unwanted refunds will drain the companies by default.
Tapbots offers a subscription transfer to Ivory (their Mastodon client), providing a graceful path forward for users willing to move platforms.
money_quotes: Financially, this isn't a 'Huh, yeah, that must kinda suck' situation. It's more of an 'Oh shit, we're fucked' situation.

Twitter's kneecapping of third-party clients didn't just mean that their future revenue was gone — it meant revenue they'd already collected from App Store subscriptions would need to go back to customers in the form of prorated refunds.

Consider the gut punch of losing your job — you stop earning income. It's brutal. Now imagine that the way it worked when you get fired or laid off is that you're also suddenly on the hook to pay back the last, say, 6 months of your income.

When a landlord decides to sell or repurpose a rental property, they give tenants notice that their leases won't be renewed, months in advance. But they don't just show up in the middle of the night, mid-lease, and change the locks or bulldoze the building.

There is something noble about two longtime rivals — competitors, yes, but with nothing but deep respect and camaraderie for each other — facing this terrible cliff together, with dignity and grace, considering their users first, as ever.
The only thing Musk has successfully done with Twitter is twist it into a company in his own mold, utterly devoid of the most essential of human qualities: honesty, grace, and empathy.
They're apologizing for something that was out of their control, against their wishes, and potentially ruinous for their own companies. That's how much they respect their users.
key_insight: Musk's abrupt termination of third-party Twitter clients didn't just kill future revenue for beloved indie developers — it created an existential financial crisis by forcing them to refund already-collected subscription money, and most users don't realize declining a refund is the single most helpful thing they can do.
